title.rendered: Jobber vs Workiz (2026): Which Is Better for Contractors?

content.rendered:
Field Service Comparison

Jobber vs Workiz (2026)

Built for different workflows — here’s which one fits your service business.

Disclosure: Some links on this page are affiliate links. If you sign up through one, I earn a small commission at no extra cost to you. My recommendations don’t change based on that.

Research updated: Mar 2026Pricing: From $29/moBest for: 1–15 technician service businesses✔ COMPARISON

Try Jobber Free →

Disclosure: The Jobber link on this page is an affiliate link, which means I may earn a commission if you sign up through it at no extra cost to you. The Workiz link is a direct, non-affiliate link. My recommendation does not change based on that.

Jobber and Workiz get compared because they both serve small service businesses, but they are not trying to solve the exact same day. Jobber is built around scheduled work, recurring service, and a cleaner all-around experience for small teams. Workiz is built around call-driven dispatch, same-day jobs, and tighter phone-to-revenue visibility.

If you run a 2 to 10 person home service business, the real question is not which one has more features. It’s whether your operation feels more like a calendar or a call board. That distinction usually decides this comparison faster than any pricing table.

Does Workiz really have a free plan?

Yes. Workiz Lite is a permanently free tier that includes scheduling, invoicing, and basic CRM for up to 2 users. It’s functional enough to test the platform with real jobs before upgrading. The main limitations: no phone system, no route optimization, and no inventory management — those require the Standard ($198/month) or Pro ($325/month) plans.

Can either replace ServiceTitan?

Neither Jobber nor Workiz matches ServiceTitan’s depth in pricebook management, marketing attribution, or enterprise reporting. But for contractors under 15–20 technicians, both cover the core workflow at a fraction of ServiceTitan’s cost. Workiz comes closer to ServiceTitan in operational features (phone, dispatch, inventory). Jobber comes closer in customer-facing polish (quotes, client portal). See our ServiceTitan alternatives guide for a full comparison.

Quoting Workflow and Conversion Tracking

Jobber’s quoting system is one of the most complete in the small-business field service category. You can build multi-option quotes (good/better/best), set automatic follow-up sequences for unapproved quotes, and track your overall quote-to-job conversion rate. The Client Hub lets customers approve quotes and pay deposits online without calling your office. Workiz has quoting too, but it doesn’t support multi-option proposals or the same level of automated follow-up — you’re doing more of that manually.

Simplicity and Time to Value

Jobber is designed to be set up in a weekend. Import your customers, set up your services, connect QuickBooks, and you’re scheduling jobs. The interface is clean and intuitive — new technicians learn it in 30 minutes. Workiz is more feature-rich but also more complex. The dispatch board, phone system, and inventory modules all need configuration. If you value getting started quickly with minimal training, Jobber has a lower barrier to entry.

Client Self-Service Portal

Jobber’s Client Hub is a branded portal where customers can request work, view upcoming appointments, approve quotes, pay invoices, and see their full service history — all without downloading an app or calling your office. It’s a small feature that has outsized impact on customer experience and office workload. Workiz doesn’t offer a comparable self-serve customer portal.

Built-In Phone System and Call Management

Workiz includes an integrated VoIP phone system with call tracking, recording, and IVR (interactive voice response). Every call is logged against the customer record automatically. You can see which marketing source generated the call, listen to recordings for quality assurance, and route calls based on availability. Jobber doesn’t have a built-in phone system — you’d need a separate tool like CallRail or a VoIP provider to get comparable call management. For businesses where the phone is the primary lead channel, this is a significant advantage.

Inventory and Parts Tracking

Workiz includes inventory management — you can track parts on trucks, set reorder alerts, and tie material usage to specific jobs. For service businesses that carry significant inventory (appliance repair, locksmith, HVAC parts), this eliminates the need for a separate inventory system. Jobber doesn’t track physical inventory at any tier.

Route Optimization

Workiz includes built-in route optimization that automatically sequences your technicians’ stops for minimum drive time. For businesses running multiple techs across a metro area with 5+ stops per day, this can save 30–60 minutes of windshield time daily. Jobber shows jobs on a map but doesn’t automatically optimize routes — you’re arranging the sequence manually.

Jobber wins on simplicity, quoting workflow, and customer self-service. It’s the best choice for contractors who want a clean, easy-to-learn tool that covers scheduling, quoting, invoicing, and payments without unnecessary complexity. The $29/month Core plan is one of the best entry points in the category.

Workiz wins on operational features — phone system, inventory, route optimization, and dispatch tools. It’s built for dispatch-heavy operations (3–20 techs) that run high volumes of calls and jobs across multiple service areas. The free Lite tier is genuinely usable for testing.

If you quote a lot of custom work and value a polished client experience, lean Jobber. If you run a high-volume, dispatch-heavy operation and want your phone system and scheduling in one place, lean Workiz. For full breakdowns, see our Jobber review and Workiz review.

Quick comparison

Feature Jobber Workiz
Best fit Scheduled and recurring service businesses Call-driven, reactive dispatch businesses
Starting price ~$29/mo annual (Core) ~$65/mo annual (Standard) for 2 users
Free trial 14 days 7 days
Scheduling style Calendar-based planning Dispatch board for live same-day work
Built-in phone system No Yes, with call tracking and recording
Client portal Stronger self-serve client experience Less central to the product
Text-to-pay Available, but less core to the workflow Stronger point-of-service collection flow
Marketing attribution Basic Better call and lead-source visibility
Integrations / ecosystem Broader ecosystem and cleaner fit for general FSM use More specialized around phone + dispatch
Default pick for most small contractors Yes No — best for the right workflow

Where Jobber wins

🗓️

Workflow fit

Better for scheduled and recurring work

If your week is planned in advance — landscaping routes, recurring cleaning, pest control, maintenance visits — Jobber’s calendar-first workflow is the cleaner fit. It feels more natural when your day is built around appointments instead of inbound urgency.

✨

Ease of use

Cleaner interface and easier onboarding

Jobber is usually the safer recommendation for small contractors because the interface is easier to learn, the daily flow is less chaotic, and new hires generally get comfortable faster. That matters when you want software that helps immediately instead of becoming its own project.

👤

Customer experience

Stronger client portal and self-serve flow

Jobber’s Client Hub is better for ongoing customer relationships. Clients can view estimates, approve work, and pay invoices through a more polished self-service experience. That’s especially useful for recurring service businesses with repeat customers.

🔗

General-purpose FSM

Broader ecosystem and more flexible default choice

If you want an all-around field service platform rather than a dispatch-and-phone specialist, Jobber is the better default. It integrates more naturally into the broader small-contractor software stack and works across more service business types without feeling overly specialized.

Try Jobber Free for 14 Days →

Where Workiz wins

📞

Phone system

Built-in calling, tracking, and recording

This is Workiz’s biggest edge. If inbound calls drive revenue, the built-in phone system matters. Calls are logged to the customer record, recording is available, and you get tighter visibility from first call to closed job without stitching together a separate VoIP stack.

🚚

Dispatch

Better for reactive, same-day operations

Workiz’s dispatch board is better when the day changes constantly. Locksmiths, garage door companies, appliance repair shops, and emergency HVAC or plumbing teams often work from live inbound demand, not a tidy weekly calendar. Workiz reflects that reality better.

💬

Collection flow

Stronger text-to-pay at point of service

For single-visit service calls, Workiz’s text-to-pay workflow is a real operational advantage. The tech finishes the job, sends a payment link, and gets paid before leaving. That’s a better fit for immediate collection than a portal-centered model.

📈

Attribution

Better visibility into calls, lead sources, and revenue

If you care about which ads or phone leads are actually turning into revenue, Workiz gives you a clearer picture. That makes it more attractive for call-driven shops spending money on Google Ads, LSAs, or other lead-gen channels.

Final verdict

Our call

Jobber for most contractors. Workiz if your business runs through the phone.

Choose Jobber if…

Jobber

You run scheduled or recurring work, want a polished all-around field service platform, and care more about ease of use, client experience, and general reliability than advanced phone features. For most small home service businesses, this is the safer default.

Choose Workiz if…

Workiz

Your business is driven by inbound calls, same-day dispatch, and on-the-spot payment collection. If your day starts with the phone ringing and jobs reshuffling in real time, Workiz is built more specifically for that operating model.

Try Jobber Free →
Visit Workiz (direct, non-affiliate) →

FAQ

Is Jobber or Workiz better for a small service business?

For most small home service businesses, Jobber is the safer default. It is easier to learn, handles scheduled and recurring work well, and has a cleaner overall experience. Workiz is the better choice if your business is driven by inbound phone calls and same-day dispatch — think locksmiths, garage door companies, or emergency plumbing and HVAC shops where the day changes constantly.

Which is cheaper, Jobber or Workiz?

Jobber starts lower at about $29/month on the Core plan for one user. Workiz’s Standard plan starts at about $65/month billed annually and includes two users. At the entry level, Jobber is less expensive. But pricing scales differently as you add users and features, so the real comparison depends on your team size and which plan tier you actually need.

Does Workiz have a built-in phone system?

Yes, and it is Workiz’s biggest differentiator. The built-in phone system includes call tracking, recording, and logging calls directly to the customer record. If inbound calls drive your revenue and you want tighter visibility from first call to closed job, that feature alone can be the deciding factor. Jobber does not have a comparable built-in phone system.

Is Jobber better for landscapers than Workiz?

Yes, in most cases. Jobber’s calendar-based scheduling, recurring job support, and client portal are a better fit for landscaping companies that plan their week in advance. Workiz is built more for reactive, call-driven businesses where the schedule changes throughout the day. Unless your landscaping business is unusually dispatch-heavy, Jobber is the more natural choice.
